ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code identified for battered fried chicken wings; omitted to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Vietnamese battered fried chicken wings is 3–4 pieces (approximately 200 g), commonly served as a snack or side dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy — Chicken wing, fried, batter-coated (NDB No. 05062)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry for batter-coated fried chicken wings (NDB 05062), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for this Vietnamese preparation. Input values from GrabFood (confidence 0.7) were cross-checked against USDA SR Legacy data; all core macros and secondary nutrients are consistent within the expected range for battered deep-fried chicken wings. USDA SR Legacy values are used as the authoritative basis. Sodium at 450 mg/100g is consistent with Vietnamese fish-sauce marinade and falls within the plausible range documented for similar preparations. Calcium (15 mg), iron (1.0 mg), cholesterol (90 mg), saturated fat (6.0 g), trans fat (0.25 g), and vitamin C (0.0 mg) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data for this food category. No exact Vietnamese-specific battered fried chicken wing entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024.

gi_source.title: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281–2283. Analogous estimate for battered deep-fried chicken with wheat-flour and cornstarch coating via University of Sydney GI Database.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese battered fried chicken wings (Cánh Gà Chiên Bột). GI estimated at 65 (medium) based on the closest analogous food: battered deep-fried chicken with wheat-flour and cornstarch coating, referenced from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. (2008) Diabetes Care 31(12):2281–2283. The batter (wheat flour + cornstarch) is the primary glycaemic driver; the chicken component contributes negligible GI. GL per serving calculated as: GI (65) × carbohydrate per serving (25.0 g from 200 g portion) / 100 = 16.3.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 450 mg/100g; estimated 900 mg per 200 g serving
hypertension.guidance: A 200 g serving delivers approximately 900 mg sodium — nearly 40% of the WHO 2,300 mg daily limit — primarily from fish sauce marinade and seasoned batter; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 1–2 wings and avoid additional dipping sauces to reduce cumulative sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Refined wheat-flour and cornstarch batter (carbohydrate 12.5 g/100g, fibre only 0.5 g/100g) with high total fat (22.5 g/100g) impairing insulin sensitivity
type2_diabetes.guidance: The refined batter coating produces a medium-to-high glycaemic response (estimated GI 65) and the high fat content (22.5 g/100g) from deep-frying further impairs postprandial insulin sensitivity; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit intake to 1–2 wings per occasion and pair with non-starchy vegetables to moderate glycaemic load.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 6.0 g/100g, trans fat 0.25 g/100g, cholesterol 90 mg/100g, and sodium 450 mg/100g
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 200 g serving contributes 12 g saturated fat (60% of the 20 g daily limit), 0.5 g trans fat, 180 mg cholesterol, and 900 mg sodium — a combination that raises LDL cholesterol, promotes arterial inflammation, and elevates blood pressure; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional food and limit to 1–2 wings per serving.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs); saturated fat 6.0 g/100g exceeds the 5 g/100g RA threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-frying generates AGEs that directly promote joint inflammation, and saturated fat at 6.0 g/100g exceeds the recommended RA threshold; individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request baked or air-fried wings and avoid fish-sauce-heavy marinades to reduce sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Cánh Gà Chiên Bột (Vietnamese Battered Fried Chicken Wings) are a popular Vietnamese snack and side dish consisting of chicken wings marinated in fish sauce, garlic, and pepper, then coated in a seasoned batter of wheat flour and cornstarch before being deep-fried until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior, juicy interior, and a savoury, slightly umami flavour profile characteristic of Vietnamese street food and casual dining. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 325 kcal, 17.5 g protein, 22.5 g fat (of which 6.0 g is saturated and 0.25 g is trans fat), 12.5 g carbohydrate, 1.5 g sugar, 0.5 g dietary fibre, and 450 mg sodium. The high sodium content is largely attributable to the fish sauce marinade. Clinically, this dish is of high concern for individuals with hypertension (900 mg sodium per 200 g serving), Type 2 Diabetes (refined batter with estimated GI 65 and high fat impairing insulin sensitivity), and cardiovascular disease (elevated saturated fat, trans fat, cholesterol, and sodium). The dish is not suitable for rheumatoid arthritis patients due to AGE formation during deep-frying. It is commonly served as a snack, appetiser, or side dish in Vietnamese restaurants and street food stalls, and is widely available on food delivery platforms in Singapore and Vietnam.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy data for batter-coated fried chicken wings (NDB 05062), which is the closest approved-source equivalent. The input GrabFood values (confidence 0.7) are consistent with USDA SR Legacy data and have been retained with USDA as the authoritative basis. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework as documented in Atkinson et al. (2008). No exact Vietnamese-specific battered fried chicken wing entry exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases as of 2024.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for Vietnamese battered fried chicken wings is not separately reported in USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 05062) at the level of precision required; these values are not available from any approved source for this specific preparation. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: these micronutrient values are not reported in the input record and are not available with sufficient specificity for this Vietnamese preparation variant in approved sources consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy NDB 05062, HPB Singapore Foodo) as of 2024. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed as effectively zero for deep-fried chicken wings; heat from deep-frying destroys residual vitamin C, and chicken wings contain negligible vitamin C in any preparation — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy. 4. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) does not correspond to a valid HPB Singapore Foodo nutrient database entry and has been replaced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the authoritative approved source. 5. Ethnic tags in the input (Pho, Banh Mi, Noodle) were removed as they are not relevant to this dish. 6. country_of_origin corrected from Singapore to Vietnam; the dish is Vietnamese in origin though sold in Singapore. 7. Nutrient source is USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (https://fdc.nal.usda.gov/), an approved secondary global source under Project Veritas v2 source policy. GI source is University of Sydney GI Database (https://www.glycemicindex.com/) with peer-reviewed journal support (Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care), both approved GI-specific sources.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodOn URI provided for fried chicken wing (FOODON_03301116). FoodEx2 code A0CKW used with exact match type, directly describing 'Chicken wings, battered and deep-fried'. SNOMED CT honestly set to null with gap note. No codes invented.

Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly with machine key 'type2_diabetes'.
